serving.natural_language_portion: A standard add-on serving of Hokkien mee wheat noodle is approximately 100 g of cooked noodle, equivalent to a small bowl or a single portion added to a Yong Tau Foo or laksa dish.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, wheat, cooked (IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia)
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry, USDA #20113 and cooked wheat noodle analogues)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Hokkien mee wheat noodle (add-on portion) in a single approved source. Values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA cooked wheat noodle data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked plain wheat noodles (NDB 20113 / FDC ID 169730). Hokkien mee wheat noodles are thick, alkaline-treated wheat noodles; nutrient profile is consistent with cooked plain wheat noodle per 100 g. Values represent the plain cooked noodle without sauce or toppings.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Wheat noodles, cooked (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value specific to Hokkien mee wheat noodle found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is taken from the closest analogous food: cooked wheat noodles (GI=52, low category) as reported in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008', Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 28.5) / 100 = 14.8 (medium GL for a 100 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content in plain cooked noodle
hypertension.guidance: Plain Hokkien mee wheat noodle contains approximately 5 mg sodium per 100 g and poses minimal hypertension risk on its own; however, sodium load increases substantially when served with high-sodium broths, sauces, or Yong Tau Foo soup bases, so patients with hypertension should monitor total dish sodium rather than the noodle component alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 52) and medium glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 52 (low-medium boundary) and a glycemic load of approximately 14.8 per 100 g serving, Hokkien mee wheat noodle produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size, pair the noodle with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt glycemic response, and avoid high-sugar or high-starch sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and negligible cholesterol in plain cooked noodle
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain Hokkien mee wheat noodle is very low in saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) and contains negligible cholesterol, making the noodle component itself a low cardiovascular risk food; cardiovascular risk in a complete Hokkien mee dish is primarily driven by the cooking oil, pork lard, or high-sodium sauces added during preparation, which should be minimised for cardiovascular patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hokkien mee wheat noodle (add-on) refers to a portion of thick, round, yellow wheat noodles served as an add-on component in Singaporean and Malaysian hawker dishes, most commonly Yong Tau Foo, laksa, or Hokkien mee stir-fry. The noodles are made from wheat flour, water, salt, and lye water (an alkaline solution), which gives them their characteristic yellow colour, springy texture, and slightly chewy bite. They are typically pre-cooked by blanching or boiling and then added to the customer's chosen dish. In the context of field survey data, this item is listed as an add-on at a Yong Tau Foo stall, allowing diners to supplement their soup or dry noodle bowl with an extra portion of Hokkien mee wheat noodle. Nutritionally, per 100 g of cooked noodle, the food provides approximately 138 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 0.6 g fat, and 28.5 g carbohydrate, with a low-to-moderate glycemic index of 52. The sodium content of the plain noodle is very low (approximately 5 mg/100 g), though total dish sodium depends heavily on the accompanying broth or sauce. The noodle is suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets and is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. It is not suitable for individuals with gluten intolerance or coeliac disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, portion control and pairing with fibre-rich vegetables are recommended to moderate the glycemic response.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Hokkien mee wheat noodle (add-on) found in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy cooked wheat noodle data, which are the closest defensible analogues. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked wheat noodles, GI=52) per the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. This record represents the plain cooked Hokkien mee wheat noodle add-on portion only, without any sauce, broth, or toppings. Nutrient values will differ substantially in a complete dish context. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for plain cooked wheat noodle per USDA SR Legacy; naturally zero by product composition). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plain wheat noodle contains no animal-derived cholesterol). 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plain cooked wheat noodle contains negligible vitamin C, confirmed per USDA SR Legacy). 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plain wheat noodle is a plant-derived product containing no vitamin B12). 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy source entries for plain cooked wheat noodle. 7. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the adapted sources for plain cooked wheat noodle. 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source (FAO/INFOODS SEA) for this food category. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for plain cooked wheat noodle. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for plain cooked wheat noodle. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this food. 12. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from cooked wheat noodles per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Hokkien mee wheat noodle was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 13. Cuisine is classified as Malaysian per input source; this noodle type is also widely used in Singaporean hawker cuisine. 14. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the Singapore hawker context of the source record.
